items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон.
items.translation:
Once again I’m tormented
by vague misgivings...
Shpak’s missing a tape recorder,
the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Largely successful: it preserves the mock-heroic phrasing (“tormented by vague misgivings”), which is the core comedic register. It also correctly encodes the loss/absence (“missing”), aligning with the reference note that the items are not possessed anymore. Minor stylistic nuance: “haunt me” is a bit more idiomatic/lofty than “I’m tormented,” but the delivered line still reads elevated and theatrical enough for the joke to land.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—very pleased.
Tsar—very pleased. Tsar.
Very pleased. Hello, Tsar.

Tsar—very pleased.
Hello, Tsar! Very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Works well: the humor is the repetitive, banal politeness and the absurd insistence on “Tsar” as if it were a social introduction. The translation keeps the staccato repetition and awkward etiquette. Slight loss: it omits the explicit “to meet you” in the first clause, but the repeated “very pleased” still conveys the same Soviet-formula greeting vibe and drunken loopiness.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Essentially identical in meaning and force; preserves the sudden authoritarian, pompous demand. No meaningful loss.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали.
items.translation: Leave me, old woman—I'm in mourning.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: The line should sound archaic/high-register and melodramatically regal. “Leave me, old woman” is good, but “I’m in mourning” shifts the meaning: mourning implies bereavement/death-specific grief, while “в печали” is broader “in sorrow/sadness.” It still reads elevated-ish, but the semantic narrowing changes the tone and can miscue the joke (he’s not mourning someone; he’s just grandly melancholic).

items.translation:
Bricked in!
Bricked in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Good comedic effect and clear situation: he feels “walled/bricked in” and blames “demons.” The English is punchy and performative. Minor nuance: the Russian has a drawn-out, lamenting repetition with ellipsis; the translation’s exclamation-heavy delivery is slightly more abrupt, but still fits panic/archaic superstition.

items.translation: So that’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and functional: keeps the religious framing and the causal attribution to the cross. Slightly less “proclamatory” than “Behold the power…”, but still clearly archaic/solemn enough to carry the joke in an atheist-modern setting.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed (a command for all to dance), but the reference analysis stresses a specific showman/MC formula (“the floor is open…”) that heightens the anachronistic ‘Soviet party host’ vibe. “Everybody dance!” is more generic and less characterful; it loses some of the comic ‘announcer’ flavor, though comprehension remains intact.

items.translation: I picked a good time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Good: idiomatic, upbeat, and self-satisfied; matches the burglar’s cheerful opportunism. “Drop by” fits the casual tone. No real loss of humor.

items.original: Икра черная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar!

Overseas “caviar”—eggplant spread!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Strong: it preserves the famous comedic pivot and, crucially, avoids calling eggplant spread straightforwardly “caviar” by adding “eggplant spread,” which matches the reference warning. The quotation marks around “caviar” also help signal the joke. Minor: the source has the extra beat “Да!” (“Yes!”) which adds showman rhythm; the translation drops it, but the punchline still lands.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три.
items.translation:
it’s all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Good overall: keeps the escalating inventory rhythm and the comic “domestic” qualifier. The final “—three” is preserved, maintaining the gag of counting. Minor stylistic/fluency issue: “suede jacket—three” is slightly unclear/awkward in English (it can momentarily read as if “three” modifies something else), whereas the Russian’s pause/ellipsis makes the self-correction more performative. Still understandable in context.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And you’ll be cured!

And you will be cured too!

And I will be cured too!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Meaning is preserved, but the comedic effect in the reference hinges on the calm, almost sing-song resignation and the shared “they’ll cure us” (implied psychiatric ward) with ellipsis pacing. The translation uses exclamation marks and drops the ellipses, making it more emphatic/cheery than resigned and slightly flattening the darkly comic undertone. Also shifts from impersonal “they’ll cure” to passive “you’ll be cured,” which is acceptable but less pointed about institutional ‘they.’

summary.interpretation: 2.09 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.42 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
